input_candidates.truth_value: The steel density is 7.34 g/cm³ measured by Archimedes' principle, 6.7% lower than low-carbon low-alloyed steels.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Work hardening rate (WHR) curves of QA specimens exhibited three stages based on slope: stage I (ε < 0.01) with sharp decrease related to soft phase deformation and dislocation movement; stage II (0.01 < ε < 0.15) with slight decrease attributed to competition between dislocation gliding and hardening mechanisms including TRIP effect; stage III (ε > 0.15) with slowly increasing WHR dominated by TRIP effect and possible TWIP effect.

Stacking fault energies (SFE) of austenite estimated by XRD Rietveld refinement: QA-620 ~36.50 mJ/m², QA-680 ~38.27 mJ/m², QA-740 ~33.29 mJ/m², QA-800 ~31.53 mJ/m², indicating TWIP effect should exist during deformation.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fractures: HR and IQ-1050 were not analyzed; QA specimens all show laminar tears, second cracks, and mixed cleavage/dimple features.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The uniform elongation of all QA specimens except QA-680 was almost equal to total elongation; QA-740 had maximum uniform elongation of ~62.4%.

input_candidates.truth_value: The steel is considered a typical third generation advanced high strength steel and can substitute TRIP 590T/400Y.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Austenite grain size statistics from EBSD before tensile tests showed that around 90% of austenite grains in QA specimens were smaller than 4.8 μm.

input_candidates.truth_value: Product of strength and elongation (PSE): HR 6.2 GPa%, IQ-1050 1.9 GPa%, QA-620 38.0 GPa%, QA-680 33.5 GPa%, QA-740 45.2 GPa%, QA-800 36.2 GPa%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Austenite mechanical stability constants (k) calculated from austenite volume fraction before and after tensile tests: QA-620 ~1.24, QA-680 ~0.56, QA-740 ~1.47, QA-800 ~1.50. Higher k value indicates lower austenitic stability.

input_candidates.truth_value: QA-680 exhibited necking at about 25% strain, but then a second stage of uniform elongation until failure due to delayed TRIP effect.

Work hardening rate curves show three stages: stage I sharp drop due to soft phase deformation; stage II slow decrease dominated by dislocation glide; stage III increase due to TRIP effect dominating with possible TWIP contribution.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Products of tensile strength and total elongation (PSE): HR ~6.2 GPa%, IQ-1050 ~1.9 GPa%, QA-620 ~38.0 GPa%, QA-680 ~33.5 GPa%, QA-740 ~45.2 GPa%, QA-800 ~36.2 GPa%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Grain boundary densities (length per area) from Fig. 8(b): HR - AG boundaries ~0.8, high-angle packet ~0.35, block ~0.75; IQ-1050 - AG ~0.6, block ~1.1; QA-620 - AG ~0.85, block ~0.4; QA-680 - AG ~1.25, block ~0.35; QA-740 - AG ~1.6, block ~0.25; QA-800 - AG ~1.25, block ~0.25. (units arbitrary from figure).
input_candidates.prediction_value: QA-680 exhibited necking at around 25% strain due to deficient TRIP effect, but afterward underwent a second stage of uniform elongation until failure because metastable austenite produced TRIP effect after necking.

input_candidates.truth_value: Austenite grain size statistics from EBSD: ~90% of grains <4.8 µm in all QA specimens; fine grains have higher enrichment of alloying elements and higher stability.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Grain boundary densities (relative units from EBSD): for QA specimens, austenitic grain (AG) boundary density increased with annealing temperature until 800°C then decreased, while block boundary (>45°) density decreased continuously with annealing temperature.

input_candidates.truth_value: The A1 temperature (cementite precipitation) is around 617 °C, which influenced the selection of annealing temperatures.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fracture morphology of QA specimens: mixed fracture features with cleavage in δ-ferrite (river pattern, cleavage step), quasi-cleavage, and dimples; laminar tears observed due to laminated microstructure; second cracks initiated at interfaces between δ-ferrite and γ/α phases.

input_candidates.truth_value: The peritectic reaction δ-F + L → A occurs at about 1390 °C; maximum austenite fraction ~91.4% at ~970 °C in equilibrium.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Tensile testing conditions: strain rate of 0.375 min^-1, rod-shaped sample size M10 × 60 mm with gauge length 25 mm, tensile axis parallel to rolling direction.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 9(a) XRD profiles before and after tensile test show peaks; the text describes intensity changes but no exact values.
input_candidates.prediction_value: For QA-800, new martensite formed from austenite during air cooling after annealing at 800°C, as evidenced in SEM micrographs.
